title: SEGMENT 15 — Infectious disease modeling: Zika, COVID, and the rest

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Infectious disease modeling: Zika, COVID, and the rest

One of the most important and timely applications of organoid technology has been in infectious disease modeling — using organoids to study how pathogens infect human tissue, what cellular damage they cause, and what drugs might treat them.
The classic example, and one of the most consequential, came in 2016 during the Zika virus outbreak.

Zika was causing a wave of birth defects in newborns in Brazil — particularly microcephaly, the same small-brain phenotype that Lancaster and Knoblich had modeled with their original cerebral organoid paper.
The hypothesis was that Zika virus was infecting the developing fetal brain in utero and disrupting cortical development.
But proving that hypothesis required studying how Zika interacts with developing human brain tissue, which was — for obvious reasons — not directly accessible.
Cerebral organoids made it accessible.
Several groups, working rapidly in early 2016, took cerebral organoids derived from healthy iPSCs, exposed them to Zika virus, and watched what happened.
The results, published within months, were unambiguous.
Zika virus selectively infected and killed neural progenitor cells in the organoids — the very cells responsible for generating cortical neurons.
The infected organoids showed reduced size, depleted progenitor zones, and decreased neuron production.
The cellular phenotype in the dish matched the developmental phenotype in newborns.
The mechanism was clear: Zika kills cortical progenitors, which depletes the pool of cells that should be making neurons, which produces a small brain.
This was an extraordinary demonstration of organoid utility.

In a matter of months — fast, by the standards of medical research — the field had used cerebral organoids to establish the mechanism by which a newly emerging virus was causing a public health crisis.
The same organoid systems were then used to screen for antiviral drugs, identifying compounds that could block Zika infection or rescue the affected cells.
Several drug candidates emerged from these screens that have since been developed further.
The Zika story established a template that the field has used repeatedly since.
When SARS-CoV-2 emerged in late 2019 and early 2020, organoid researchers were ready.

Within months, multiple groups had used lung organoids, intestinal organoids, liver organoids, kidney organoids, vascular organoids, and brain organoids to study how SARS-CoV-2 infects various human tissues, what cell types it targets, what damage it causes, and what drugs might block it.
The findings were illuminating.
Lung organoids showed how SARS-CoV-2 infects type II alveolar cells via the ACE2 receptor, with viral replication producing cell damage and inflammatory signaling.
Intestinal organoids revealed that the virus can infect gut epithelium too, helping explain GI symptoms and viral shedding in stool.

Vascular organoids — which we haven't discussed much, but which are derived from pluripotent cells and contain endothelial cells and vascular networks — showed direct infection of endothelium, partially explaining the vascular complications of COVID.
Brain organoids demonstrated that SARS-CoV-2 could infect neurons and glia under certain conditions, providing a possible substrate for the neurological symptoms some patients experienced.
These organoid-based findings, generated quickly during a pandemic, fed into the broader effort to understand and treat COVID.
Drug screens in organoid systems helped identify candidate antivirals, including some that were later evaluated in patients.
The general lesson here: organoids are a rapid, flexible tool for studying any pathogen that infects human tissue.
Within weeks of an emerging infection, you can have organoids of the relevant target tissue exposed to the pathogen, characterized for infection, and screened for drug response.

This is a faster turnaround than animal models (which require breeding and infrastructure) and a more human-relevant turnaround than cell line models (which often don't capture the cell types and architectures that pathogens actually target).
Some specific applications that organoids have proven useful for: Norovirus — long considered impossible to grow in culture — was finally cultured in intestinal organoids in 2016.
Rotavirus, Cryptosporidium, adenovirus, CMV, hepatitis B and C viruses, herpes simplex virus, respiratory syncytial virus — all have been studied productively in organoid systems.
Some pathogens that resist traditional culture become tractable in organoids precisely because the organoids reproduce the specific cellular environment those pathogens require.
The future here is bright.

We're heading toward a world where, when a new infectious disease emerges, organoid systems will be among the first lines of investigation, providing rapid answers about mechanism, target cells, and candidate drugs.
That capability didn't exist twenty years ago.
It does now.
